tasks.task_name: Look up eyeglasses stores in Kyoto
tasks.first_domino: Open Google Maps and search 'optician Kyoto'
tasks.hook_line: Kyoto might have smaller opticians with better prices than Tokyo — could save $200+ by shopping early.
tasks.reward: Locks in the first stop for glasses shopping and removes the Kyoto guesswork.
nuggets: 4 pairs could save $400+ vs US prices
Kyoto less touristy than Tokyo = better deals
Insurance might cover some or all
tasks.system_prompt: I'm in Kyoto right now (March 30, 2026) and want to buy 4 pairs of eyeglasses during my Japan trip. Help me find reputable optician stores in Kyoto. I need: store names, addresses, Google Maps links, whether they speak English, typical price ranges for quality frames with lenses, and hours of operation. I prefer smaller local shops over big chains if the quality is good. Output as a list with quick reference details.

tasks.task_name: Validate insurance/prescription requirements for 4 pairs of glasses
tasks.first_domino: Open insurance app and search 'vision benefits international'
tasks.hook_line: If insurance covers international purchases, you could get 4 pairs basically free — but you need to know now before buying.
tasks.reward: Removes all financial uncertainty from the glasses shopping plan.
nuggets: Some vision plans reimburse foreign purchases
May need to submit receipts manually after trip
Prescription might expire before next checkup
tasks.system_prompt: I'm in Japan and planning to buy 4 pairs of eyeglasses. Help me figure out: (1) Does my US vision insurance cover international eyeglass purchases? (2) What documentation do I need to save for reimbursement? (3) Are there restrictions on how many pairs I can claim per year? (4) Is my prescription still valid for buying glasses abroad? Give me a checklist of things to verify with my insurance company and what to ask the optician in Japan.

tasks.first_domino: Open Google Maps and create a new list 'Kyoto Must-See'
tasks.hook_line: Kyoto has 2000+ temples — which ones are worth waking up early for, and which are tourist traps?
tasks.reward: Turns Kyoto from a blank page into a clear day-by-day plan with walking routes.
nuggets: Fushimi Inari free, open 24hrs, best at dawn
Philosopher's Path: 2km temple walk, cherry blossoms
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan activities for Kyoto (currently there, March 30 onward). Traveling with my girlfriend Dana. We like temples, walking, outdoor spaces, onsen, and good food. We're staying near central Kyoto. I want a day-by-day itinerary with: temple/site names, opening hours, entry fees, Google Maps links, and walking routes that connect multiple stops efficiently. Mix major landmarks with hidden gems. Flag which spots are worth early wake-ups to avoid crowds. Include food recommendations near each cluster of activities.

tasks.first_domino: Search 'Takayama day trips from town center'
tasks.hook_line: Takayama is the Japanese Alps — rural mountain town vibes, morning markets, and nearby hiking trails you've never heard of.
tasks.reward: Fills in the mountain portion of the trip with concrete activities and transportation details.
nuggets: Old town district: Edo-era streets, sake breweries
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan activities for Takayama in the Japanese Alps. We'll be there for 2-3 days coming from Kyoto. Interests: mountain views, hiking, old town architecture, onsen, local food. I want: a list of must-see spots in Takayama town itself, day trip options (with bus/train times and costs), hiking trails accessible without a car, and onsen recommendations nearby. Include morning market details and any seasonal considerations for late March/early April.

tasks.first_domino: Open Google Maps and create a new list 'Tokyo Neighborhoods'
tasks.hook_line: Tokyo is massive — can you build a plan that hits Shibuya, teamLab, stationery shops, and limited edition fountain pens without burning out?
tasks.reward: Turns the final week into a clear neighborhood-by-neighborhood plan with shopping targets locked in.
nuggets: Shibuya teamLab Borderless needs advance tickets
Itoya flagship: 12 floors of stationery in Ginza
Sailor limited editions at Kingdom Note Shinjuku
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan activities for Tokyo (arriving around April 1-2, staying until April 7). Traveling with Dana. Interests: stationery/fountain pen shops, teamLab museum, interesting neighborhoods, street food, less touristy experiences. Specific shopping goals: Sailor fountain pen limited editions, dot grid disc journal paper. I want a neighborhood-based plan (e.g., 'Shibuya day', 'Ginza day') with: shop names/addresses, opening hours, walking routes, food recommendations, and non-shopping activities mixed in. Include advance booking requirements for teamLab and any other reservations needed.

tasks.first_domino: Clone the Cerebro repo and open README.md
tasks.hook_line: Developer portals are supposed to make engineers' lives easier — can you get this one running before it becomes a meme?
tasks.reward: Unblocks all future Cerebro work and gives you a working sandbox to explore the codebase.
nuggets: Node.js project, probably needs nvm or volta
May have Docker dependencies for backend services
Check for .env.example file for config secrets
tasks.system_prompt: I'm trying to get the Cerebro developer portal running locally. It's a work project I haven't touched since February. Walk me through: (1) Typical setup steps for a Node.js developer portal (dependencies, environment variables, database/service requirements). (2) Common pitfalls when running developer portals locally (auth mocks, API keys, CORS issues). (3) How to verify it's working (which routes to hit, what should render). (4) Debugging checklist if it doesn't start. Assume I have Node/npm/Docker but nothing else pre-configured.

recommended_slots.reason: You're in Kyoto right now and glasses shopping is time-sensitive — finding stores today means you can visit tomorrow.
Kyoto planning is high-impact for the current leg of your trip, and you have existing momentum from being on the ground.
Quick insurance check before glasses shopping — removes financial risk and takes under 20 minutes.
